title: Sorting
description: Built-in sorting, custom comparators, and sort-based interview patterns.

sections.title: Built-in sort
sections.explanation: Every language has O(n log n) built-in sort. Know both in-place and copy variants.

snippets.python:
nums = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5]

# In-place sort
nums.sort()              # [1, 1, 3, 4, 5]

# Return new sorted list (original unchanged)
sorted_nums = sorted(nums)

# Reverse sort
nums.sort(reverse=True)  # [5, 4, 3, 1, 1]

sections.title: Custom comparators
sections.explanation: Sort by a custom key — most interview sorting problems need this. Sort by second element, by string length, by frequency, etc.

snippets.python:
# Sort by second element
intervals = [[1,3], [2,1], [4,2]]
intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])

# Sort by length
words = ["banana", "pie", "kiwi"]
words.sort(key=len)

# Sort by multiple keys
students = [("Alice", 90), ("Bob", 90), ("Charlie", 85)]
students.sort(key=lambda x: (-x[1], x[0]))
# Sort by grade descending, then name ascending

sections.title: Interview Pattern: Merge Intervals
sections.explanation: Sort intervals by start, then merge overlapping ones. A top-10 interview pattern.

sections.title: Gotcha: JavaScript default sort is lexicographic
sections.explanation: JavaScript's default .sort() converts elements to strings. This breaks numeric sorting. Always pass a comparator.
